learningOutcomes: Identify the main idea and key details in a short passage
Answer literal questions by locating information directly in the text
Understand the meaning of unfamiliar words using context clues
realLifeContexts: Reading a news article and summarising what happened
Following instructions in a recipe or manual
Understanding a notice or announcement at school
commonMisconceptions: The main idea is always the first sentence of a passage
Every word in a passage is equally important

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between explicit and implicit information in a text
Make inferences and draw conclusions supported by textual evidence
Identify the author's purpose and tone
Summarise a passage accurately in your own words
realLifeContexts: Analysing a newspaper opinion column to identify bias
Reading a product review and judging its reliability
Interpreting a job advertisement to understand requirements
Evaluating conflicting reports on the same news event
commonMisconceptions: Inferences are just guesses with no basis in the text
The author's opinion is always clearly stated

learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the reliability and validity of arguments in a text
Analyse how language choices create meaning and influence the reader
Compare perspectives across multiple texts on the same topic
Identify rhetorical techniques and assess their effectiveness
realLifeContexts: Evaluating the credibility of sources for an academic essay
Analysing political speeches for persuasive techniques
Comparing media coverage of a controversial event
Reading legal or contractual documents critically
commonMisconceptions: A well-written text is always reliable and unbiased
Critical reading means finding fault with everything

learningOutcomes: Structure a short essay with an introduction, body, and conclusion
Write a clear topic sentence for each paragraph
Use basic linking words to connect ideas (firstly, however, in conclusion)
realLifeContexts: Writing a short piece about your favourite hobby
Describing a place you have visited
Expressing an opinion about a school rule
commonMisconceptions: An essay conclusion must introduce new ideas
Longer sentences always make writing better

learningOutcomes: Write a well-structured argumentative or discursive essay
Use a range of cohesive devices to build coherent paragraphs
Support claims with specific evidence and examples
Adapt tone and register for different essay types (narrative, descriptive, argumentative)
realLifeContexts: Writing a persuasive letter to a local council about a community issue
Crafting a college application personal statement
Writing a review of a film or book for a school magazine
Composing a formal report on a school survey
commonMisconceptions: Persuasive essays only need one side of an argument
Using complex vocabulary always improves an essay

learningOutcomes: Construct a nuanced argument that acknowledges complexity and ambiguity
Employ a wide range of stylistic devices to enhance clarity and impact
Integrate and cite sources accurately within an extended essay
Evaluate and refine writing through multiple drafts
realLifeContexts: Writing an extended research essay for university entry
Composing an editorial for a national newspaper
Drafting a policy proposal for a school or community organisation
Writing a critical analysis of a work of literature
commonMisconceptions: A sophisticated essay must use formal language exclusively
Once a thesis is stated it cannot be refined during the writing process

learningOutcomes: Identify and use nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs correctly
Construct grammatically correct simple and compound sentences
Use basic punctuation: full stops, commas, question marks, and capital letters
realLifeContexts: Correcting errors in a text message before sending
Writing a clear and accurate note to a friend
Filling out a form with correct spelling and punctuation
commonMisconceptions: A sentence must always be long to be grammatically correct
Adverbs can only modify verbs

learningOutcomes: Use complex and compound-complex sentence structures accurately
Apply correct subject-verb agreement in varied constructions
Use tenses consistently and appropriately across a piece of writing
Identify and correct common errors: run-ons, fragments, and misplaced modifiers
realLifeContexts: Proofreading a job application letter for grammatical errors
Editing a classmate's essay to improve clarity
Writing formal emails with correct grammar and tone
Analysing grammar in published newspaper articles
commonMisconceptions: You should never start a sentence with 'And' or 'But'
Passive voice is always incorrect and should be avoided

learningOutcomes: Manipulate syntactic structures to achieve specific rhetorical effects
Analyse how grammar choices shape meaning and style in literary texts
Apply grammar rules accurately in academic and creative writing contexts
Evaluate language change and variation across dialects and registers
realLifeContexts: Analysing how a politician's sentence structure builds authority
Understanding how advertising language manipulates through grammar
Adapting language register for legal, academic, and creative contexts
Studying how language evolves in social media and digital communication
commonMisconceptions: Standard grammar rules are universal across all varieties of English
Grammar and style are entirely separate concerns

learningOutcomes: Write a short story with a clear beginning, middle, and end
Create a simple character with basic physical and personality traits
Use descriptive language to bring a setting to life
realLifeContexts: Writing a diary entry about an exciting day
Describing a dream or imagined adventure
Creating a short story inspired by a photograph
commonMisconceptions: A good story must always have a happy ending
Dialogue is just conversation written down with no special rules

learningOutcomes: Structure a narrative with rising action, climax, and resolution
Develop a complex character whose motivations drive the plot
Use show-don't-tell techniques to convey emotion and atmosphere
Vary sentence structure and pace to create tension and interest
realLifeContexts: Writing a short story for a school or national competition
Crafting a personal narrative for a college application
Writing a script for a short film or play
Creating a blog post that tells a compelling personal story
commonMisconceptions: The climax must always occur at the very end of the story
Every detail in a narrative must directly advance the plot

learningOutcomes: Experiment with non-linear narrative structures and multiple perspectives
Use literary techniques (symbolism, motif, irony) deliberately and effectively
Craft a distinctive authorial voice through consistent stylistic choices
Critically evaluate and revise creative work using peer and self-assessment
realLifeContexts: Writing a short story for literary magazine submission
Developing the opening chapter of a novel
Writing a creative non-fiction piece on a social issue
Crafting a personal essay in the vein of published essayists
commonMisconceptions: Experimental structure means the story has no rules
Voice is a fixed quality that cannot be developed with practice

learningOutcomes: Identify basic poetic features: rhyme, rhythm, and repetition
Understand the literal meaning of a poem
Describe the mood or feeling a poem creates
realLifeContexts: Listening to song lyrics and identifying rhyme patterns
Reading a poem on a greeting card
Reciting a nursery rhyme and noticing its musical quality
commonMisconceptions: All poems must rhyme
Poetry is only about feelings and cannot tell a story

learningOutcomes: Identify and analyse figurative language: simile, metaphor, personification
Explain how structure and form contribute to meaning
Analyse the effect of sound devices: alliteration, assonance, onomatopoeia
Interpret the theme of a poem using textual evidence
realLifeContexts: Analysing the lyrics of a protest song for social commentary
Comparing how two poets describe the same topic differently
Writing an analysis of a war poem for Remembrance Day
Identifying metaphors in advertising slogans
commonMisconceptions: Identifying a technique is enough — no explanation of effect is needed
The poet always shares the same views as the poem's speaker

learningOutcomes: Conduct a close reading that integrates analysis of form, structure, and language
Compare how poets from different contexts approach a shared theme
Evaluate the influence of historical and cultural context on a poem's meaning
Construct a sustained, evidence-based literary argument about a poem
realLifeContexts: Analysing a contemporary poem published in a literary journal
Comparing colonial and post-colonial poetry perspectives
Studying how slam poetry addresses modern social issues
Writing a comparative essay for a university entrance exam
commonMisconceptions: There is one correct interpretation of every poem
Form and structure are less important than language features

learningOutcomes: Identify similes and metaphors in a text
Recognise personification and explain its effect
Understand why writers use figurative language
realLifeContexts: Noticing similes in sports commentary ('fast as lightning')
Identifying metaphors in song lyrics
Recognising personification in children's stories
commonMisconceptions: A simile and a metaphor mean exactly the same thing
Figurative language always makes writing harder to understand

learningOutcomes: Analyse the effect of irony, hyperbole, and symbolism in a text
Explain how literary devices contribute to tone and atmosphere
Identify foreshadowing and flashback and explain their structural purpose
Use a range of literary devices effectively in your own writing
realLifeContexts: Recognising dramatic irony in a film or TV series
Spotting hyperbole in everyday speech ('I've told you a million times')
Analysing the symbolism of flags or colour in public life
Identifying foreshadowing in a thriller novel
commonMisconceptions: Irony simply means something is funny or coincidental
Symbols always have a single fixed meaning regardless of context

learningOutcomes: Evaluate how writers use an extended metaphor or allegory to convey a sustained argument
Analyse the interaction of multiple literary devices within a single passage
Compare the use of literary devices across different genres and periods
Assess how literary devices shape the reader's emotional and intellectual response
realLifeContexts: Analysing Animal Farm as a political allegory
Studying how dystopian fiction uses allegory to comment on society
Tracing a motif (e.g. light/darkness) through an entire novel
Evaluating how advertising uses juxtaposition to sell an idea
commonMisconceptions: Every element of a text is a deliberate literary device
Identifying more devices in an analysis always produces a better answer

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between formal and informal writing styles
Write a short formal letter with correct layout and salutation
Use formal vocabulary and avoid slang or colloquialisms
realLifeContexts: Writing a letter to a teacher requesting permission for leave
Composing a polite email to a shop to complain about a product
Filling in a formal application form correctly
commonMisconceptions: Formal writing must always use the longest and most complex words
Formal and polite mean exactly the same thing

learningOutcomes: Write a formal report or proposal with appropriate sections and headings
Craft a persuasive formal letter that balances evidence and tone
Adapt writing style and vocabulary to different professional contexts
Use hedging language and cautious tone appropriately in formal writing
realLifeContexts: Writing a report on a school event for the headteacher
Drafting a proposal for a community project
Writing a formal complaint letter to a service provider
Composing a covering letter for a job application
commonMisconceptions: Formal reports must use passive voice throughout
A covering letter should repeat everything already on a CV

learningOutcomes: Produce a polished professional document tailored to a specific audience and purpose
Write abstractly and precisely about complex or technical subjects
Evaluate the effectiveness of formal writing using clear criteria
Navigate the conventions of academic, legal, and business writing registers
realLifeContexts: Drafting a business case for investment
Writing a legal brief or memorandum
Composing an academic journal article abstract
Preparing official correspondence on behalf of an organisation
commonMisconceptions: Formal writing cannot have personality or voice
Technical accuracy and clarity are competing goals

learningOutcomes: Deliver a short prepared speech on a familiar topic clearly and audibly
Maintain eye contact and use appropriate body language while speaking
Listen actively and respond to a speaker's point
realLifeContexts: Introducing yourself to a new class or group
Presenting a short project to the class
Speaking in a school assembly
prerequisites: Confidence to speak in front of others
commonMisconceptions: Speaking fast shows confidence and intelligence
Good speakers never use notes or preparation

learningOutcomes: Structure a persuasive speech with a clear argument and supporting evidence
Use rhetorical devices (tricolon, rhetorical question, anaphora) to engage an audience
Rebut an opposing argument logically and respectfully in a debate
Adjust language and tone for different formal and informal speaking contexts
realLifeContexts: Debating a school policy change in a student council meeting
Delivering a TEDx-style talk on a topic you care about
Participating in a mock parliament or model United Nations
Presenting a business idea in a pitch competition
commonMisconceptions: Winning a debate means shouting the loudest or speaking the most
Rhetorical questions must always be answered by the speaker

learningOutcomes: Construct and deliver a sophisticated argument that anticipates and addresses counter-arguments
Analyse the rhetorical strategies of influential public speakers
Manage a formal debate as chair or timekeeper using correct procedure
Adapt communication style in real time based on audience reaction
realLifeContexts: Competing in a British Parliamentary or World Schools debate tournament
Delivering a valedictorian or commencement address
Advocating for a cause in a public hearing or town hall
Analysing famous speeches (Churchill, King, Obama) for rhetorical technique
commonMisconceptions: Ethos, pathos, and logos must be used in equal measure in every speech
Extemporaneous speaking means speaking without any preparation
